subject: Dev Scout 2026-W28 - 5 source-backed ways to ship faster and safer
body_text:
Hi Serhii,

I rebuilt this week's Dev Scout run from primary docs and real corroboration after discarding a thinner placeholder draft.

1. OpenAI Evals meta-evals plus PR gating (robustness)
   Source: https://github.com/openai/evals/blob/main/docs/build-eval.md
   Try Monday: Turn one fragile prompt workflow into a 20-25 sample eval before the next prompt or model tweak.

2. AI Gateway provider sorting by cost, TTFT, or TPS (speed)
   Source: https://vercel.com/docs/ai-gateway/models-and-providers/provider-filtering-and-ordering
   Try Monday: Switch one interactive coding endpoint to `ttft` sorting and compare perceived responsiveness.

3. AI Gateway model fallbacks without app-side retry code (robustness)
   Source: https://vercel.com/docs/ai-gateway/models-and-providers/model-fallbacks
   Try Monday: Add one backup model to a non-critical route and capture fallback metadata in logs.

4. Scheduled Claude Code workflows with repo-scoped subagents (both)
   Source: https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/claude-code/github-actions
   Try Monday: Schedule one daily maintenance workflow with a dedicated subagent.

5. Roots-aware MCP filesystem server pattern (both)
   Source: https://github.com/modelcontextprotocol/servers
   Try Monday: Add a filesystem MCP server to one sandbox repo and keep it pinned to a single folder first.

Bonus:
- Codex CLI review-before-push workflow: https://developers.openai.com/codex/cli

Full digest: runs/2026-W28/05-report/weekly-digest.md

top_items.title: OpenAI Evals meta-evals plus PR gating
top_items.why: The useful part of Evals is not just running a dataset once - it is adding labeled meta-evals and a pull-request gate so regressions are caught before merge.
how_to_steps: Put your dataset in `evals/registry/data/<eval_name>/samples.jsonl` and register it in `evals/registry/evals/<eval_name>.yaml`.
Run `oaieval <model> <eval_name>` locally to make sure the task and rubric behave the way you expect.
For model-graded evals, add labeled choice data and a meta-eval so the reported `metascore/` accuracy is close to `1.0` before you trust the grader.
Copy the repository's PR workflow pattern so new eval YAMLs automatically run a lightweight `oaieval ... --max_samples 10` check in CI.
top_items.evidence: The build guide explicitly recommends meta-evals and says good model-graded evals should report `metascore/` accuracy close to `1.0`; the repo workflow automatically runs new eval YAMLs on pull requests.
top_items.try_monday: Take one fragile prompt workflow and turn it into a 20-25 sample eval with a meta-eval before the next model or prompt change.

top_items.title: AI Gateway provider sorting by cost, TTFT, or TPS
top_items.why: When the same model is available from multiple providers, you can route for the cheapest, fastest-first-token, or highest-throughput option without changing application code.
how_to_steps: Call the AI SDK or OpenAI-compatible API through AI Gateway rather than pinning your app to one provider endpoint.
Set `providerOptions.gateway.sort` to `cost`, `ttft`, or `tps` based on whether you want cheaper dev runs, faster interactive latency, or better long-output throughput.
Combine `sort` with `only` or `order` when you need to restrict routing to approved providers or promote a preferred vendor.
Inspect `providerMetadata.gateway.routing.sort` on responses so you can see the exact execution order, metrics, and any deprioritized providers.
top_items.evidence: The docs expose explicit sort controls for cost, median TTFT, and TPS, while the changelog explains that ranking updates at request time as provider prices and latency shift.
top_items.try_monday: Route one interactive coding assistant endpoint by `ttft` for a day and compare perceived responsiveness against your current default.

top_items.title: AI Gateway model fallbacks without app-side retry code
top_items.why: AI Gateway can fail over across both providers and backup models, so you can survive outages or model incompatibilities without building custom retry orchestration in your app.
how_to_steps: Keep your preferred model in `model`, then add a `providerOptions.gateway.models` array for the fallback order you actually want in production.
Add `order` when you also care which provider is tried first for each model, such as Azure before OpenAI or Vertex before Anthropic.
Log the returned `modelAttempts` and `providerAttempts` metadata so you can see which provider or backup model handled failures.
Test one forced-failure path before rollout so you know the fallback model still meets your latency and quality floor.
top_items.evidence: The fallback docs show ordered retries across primary and backup models and providers; Vercel's architecture post describes automatic rerouting and retries when a provider slows down or fails.
top_items.try_monday: Add one cheaper or more available backup model to a non-critical route and capture `modelAttempts` in logs.

top_items.title: Scheduled Claude Code workflows with repo-scoped subagents
top_items.why: Claude Code's GitHub Action now supports comment-triggered and scheduled automation, while still following `CLAUDE.md` and custom agents in the repo.
how_to_steps: Run `/install-github-app` for the fast path or manually install the Claude GitHub App, add `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY`, and copy a workflow into `.github/workflows/`.
Start with `anthropics/claude-code-action@v1` and use `prompt` for the task you want automated.
Move project rules into `CLAUDE.md` so the action follows the same standards your local sessions use.
For scheduled or specialized jobs, store a custom agent in `.claude/agents/` and pass `claude_args: --agent <name>` or call it explicitly from the workflow prompt.
top_items.evidence: Official docs provide quick setup, manual install, cron examples, and v1 workflow syntax; the GitHub discussion shows the `--agent` pattern for scheduled custom subagents.
top_items.try_monday: Schedule one daily maintenance workflow that reads a repo-scoped agent and summarizes stale issues or flaky tests.

top_items.title: Roots-aware MCP filesystem server pattern
top_items.why: The reference MCP servers now give you a copyable pattern for safe local tool access: explicit allowed directories, runtime roots updates, and client configs for Claude Desktop or VS Code.
how_to_steps: Start with the reference server registry, then choose the filesystem server when you need tightly scoped file access instead of a broad shell integration.
Configure the server in your MCP client with `npx -y @modelcontextprotocol/server-filesystem` or Docker and pass only the directories the agent should touch.
Prefer clients that support Roots so the allowed directories can update at runtime instead of hardcoding every path up front.
If the server fails under NVM or similar PATH managers, switch to an absolute `node` or `npx` path or a wrapper script before debugging higher-level agent behavior.
top_items.evidence: Primary docs include concrete `npx`, Docker, VS Code, and Claude Desktop configs plus Roots-based runtime directory updates; the repo is the official MCP reference server collection.
top_items.try_monday: Add one filesystem MCP server to a sandbox repo and restrict it to a single project folder before expanding access.
